Manifesto.title: A community serving Hispanidad
Manifesto.paragraph: he Escuela Hispánica is born from our conviction that the examination and understanding of our shared intellectual past and lineage, with an eye toward the future, will contribute to bringing our countries closer together and add great value to political discourse, social and cultural dialogue, and public policy development.

Project.title: PROJECT
Project.paragraph: Research on the influence of Hispanic scholasticism on US independence. A forgotten intellectual bridge between Salamanca and Philadelphia that rewrites our common history.

Membership.title: Become part of our academic and cultural community
Membership.description: The Escuela Hispánica is not just an academic institution, it is a living community. Collaborate with us and contribute to the rebirth of our legacy.

amigo.title: Friend
amigo.desc: Support our cultural mission and receive quarterly newsletters and priority access to public events.

miembro.title: Academic Member

mecenas.title: Patron
mecenas.desc: Become part of the Council of Honor. Invitations to exclusive dinners and special mention in publications.

Pillars.pillar1Desc: This axis is dedicated to the systematic reconstruction of the conceptual architecture that shaped the Hispanic political world: its legal categories, its understanding of sovereignty, the articulation between natural law and positive law, political representation, jurisdiction, and the limitation of power. The goal is to contribute to the consolidation of a corpus of ideas with shared categories, capable of engaging with contemporary political theory without reducing itself to a mere reaction against other traditions.
Pillars.pillar2Title: Hispanic Tradition in Dialogue with the West
Pillars.pillar2Desc: The Hispanic tradition did not develop in isolation, but in constant dialogue with the rest of European and Atlantic thought. This axis examines those interactions—especially with the Anglo-American tradition—to understand how the modern world was shaped by multiple converging currents. The purpose is not to establish identity-based oppositions, but to reintegrate this tradition into the common Western narrative, highlighting its contribution to contemporary debates on liberalism, political community, and international order.
Pillars.pillar3Desc: This axis explores the concrete ways in which freedom was articulated historically in the Hispanic world through institutions, charters, pacts, and mechanisms for limiting power. Rather than abstract or purely individualistic conceptions, the Hispanic experience developed an institutional and communal understanding of freedom. It also provides conceptual tools for addressing contemporary debates on social fragmentation, the crisis of institutional legitimacy, and the redefinition of the political order in the twenty-first century.

Hero.title: PROJECT
Hero.subtitle: The footprint of Hispanic Scholasticism in the US Declaration of Independence.
Hero.researchNote: Research project in development on the occasion of the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence of the United States of America (1776–2026).

Content.foundersSubtitle: Key figures of the American Revolution whose intellectual training and personal library reveal a significant connection with the scholastic tradition.
Content.introParagraph1: From Escuela Hispánica we have promoted Project 1776; an ongoing research project marking the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence of the United States of America (1776–2026). This project explores the influence of the political thought of the Second Scholasticism—especially the School of Salamanca—on the intellectual origins of American constitutionalism and the defense of individual freedom against absolute power.
Content.introParagraph2: Various authors have shown that many of the principles we today consider pillars of modern democracies and, in particular, of the American republic—such as human dignity, popular sovereignty, the limitation of power, or respect for individual conscience—were formulated centuries earlier by Catholic theologians and jurists of the 16th and 17th centuries. A key example is the famous formula of the Jesuit Francisco Suárez (1548–1617): “Omnis potestas a Deo, populum consentientem” (“All power comes from God, but through the consent of the people”). This conception of political power as delegated by the community—and not as an absolute authority granted directly to the monarch—dismantles the cliché that modern Catholic thought supported absolutism. In fact, it was often Protestant reformers, both Lutheran and Anglican, who sustained monarchical models "by the grace of God".
Content.introParagraph3: The leading exponents of the School of Salamanca, influenced by Thomas Aquinas and headed by figures such as Francisco de Vitoria, Juan de Mariana, Robert Bellarmine, and Suárez himself, developed a theological-juridical vision of the political order based on natural law, moral law, and rationality. These ideas spread through universities, seminaries, and libraries, both in the Iberian Peninsula and in Hispanic America, constituting what we might today call an authentic “Hispanic tradition of liberty”.
Content.introParagraph4: Traditionally, academic studies have focused on the impact of this tradition during the Hispanic American independence processes (1810–2010). Researchers such as Carlos Stoetzer, Fr. Furlong, or institutes and research centers like the Fe y Libertad Institute (Guatemala) have demonstrated how the scholastics influenced the emerging republican doctrines in Hispanic America, especially through the Jesuit educational network.
Content.introParagraph5: However, Project 1776 broadens this horizon to also analyze its possible influence on the independence of the United States. Although the Anglo-Saxon world did not have a scholastic institutional infrastructure as developed as in Hispanic America, there are growing indications of the circulation and reception of authors from the Hispanic tradition—especially Suárez and Bellarmine—in the British colonies. This is suggested by works such as those of Rafael Termes, recent research on George Mason, and reflections like those of Professor Karl Maurer, who noted:
Content.introQuote: “It is undeniable that Thomas Jefferson, author of the Declaration of Independence, and George Mason, author of the Virginia Declaration of Rights, were well acquainted with the great classical and contemporary thinkers since Aristotle. And it is not unreasonable to conclude that they were also familiar with writers who defended popular sovereignty and opposed the absolute power of kings.”
Content.introParagraph6: A decade ago, the president of Escuela Hispánica, Alejandro Chafuen, proposed this line of research in an article published in Forbes: “Hispanics Finding Roots and Helping Build Our America”. More recently, José Sáenz Crespo took up this issue in an interview for the Intercollegiate Studies Institute with Juan A. Soto and Enrique Pallares, where the need to rediscover a “Hispanic tradition of liberty”, unjustly forgotten in Anglo-Saxon narratives, is defended.
Content.introParagraph7: Thus, the objective of this project is not only to recover an intellectual genealogy alternative to the Enlightenment-Protestant narrative of the origins of the United States, but also to show how Catholic political philosophy—when based on the dignity of the person, natural law, and moral freedom—can contribute to the universal ideals of justice and limited government.
Content.introParagraph8: Just as in recent decades the historical role of Spain in military terms in the independence of the United States has been vindicated—let us remember that it was Bernardo de Gálvez, and not Lafayette, who marched alongside George Washington in the victory parade—it is now time to also investigate the intellectual plane: the relationship between the political traditions that contributed to the philosophical pillars of those incipient united states of North America.
Content.introParagraph9: The Hispanic tradition should not, therefore, be understood as an antagonist to the Anglo-American one, but as complementary and necessary to sustain the enormous weight of the Western project. It is a vast, rich, and chronologically earlier tradition, whose impact is increasingly evident both indirectly—through the Scottish Enlightenment—and directly on the Founding Fathers of the United States themselves. The West is not the exclusive product of a single intellectual current, but the sum of the Hispanic tradition, the Anglo-American one, and a constellation of minor continental European traditions; all of them necessary to support its moral, juridical, and political architecture. In this sense, Project 1776 aspires to vindicate the indispensable role of the Hispanic tradition, its deep connection with the Anglo-American one, and its responsibility in the intellectual and philosophical support of the West, frequently minimized over the last two centuries.
Content.introParagraph10: This is, therefore, a history to be rediscovered, on the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence and beyond.
Content.introParagraph11: Project 1776 seeks not only to recover a forgotten intellectual genealogy, but also to offer an alternative vision—Hispanic, humanist, and Catholic—of the birth of modern constitutionalism.

Axes.title: Project Axes
Axes.description: This project is organized around several operational and research axes:
Axes.01: Comparative studies between Hispanic American and United States emancipations.
Axes.02: Studies on the influence of the Hispanic tradition on the Founding Fathers of the United States.
Axes.03: Publications on the influence of the Hispanic tradition on the Anglo-American political tradition.
Axes.04: In-person and virtual events on the previous points.

Paintings.galvezMarchDesc: This painting represents General Bernardo de Gálvez leading his troops through a swamp during the American Revolutionary War campaign. Carrying both a Spanish and an American flag, Gálvez on horseback guides his men as they advance with difficulty through water and vegetation. Soldiers of different races, including Native Americans, participate in the march, reflecting the diversity and determination of the army fighting for freedom.
Paintings.forSpain: "FOR SPAIN AND FOR THE KING" - AUGUSTO FERRER-DALMAU
Paintings.forSpainDesc: The painting portrays a battle scene during the American Revolutionary War, highlighting Spanish participation.
Paintings.sisterFlagsDesc: This painting shows two majestic ships, one Spanish and one American, sailing together.

Vision.title: Vision and Foundations
Vision.text: Project 1776 seeks to dust off the Hispanic tradition, unknown or forgotten by many, update it, and place it at the service of our societies and the West, as a complementary and necessary vision to the Anglo-American one.

Participate.title: PARTICIPATE IN THE PROJECT
Participate.description: If you are a researcher, academic or wish to support this line of research for the 250th anniversary of the United States, contact us.

Hero.label: Institution
Hero.title: The School
Hero.description: The Hispanic School was founded with the aim of consolidating a space for the study, research and dissemination of the Hispanic language, history and culture. Since its creation, it has been dedicated to strengthening academic and cultural ties between Spain and Latin America.

MisionVision.mision: To promote the study and renewal of Hispanic political thought, exploring the ideas and traditions that have shaped the notion of freedom in the Hispanic world. The Hispanic School seeks to build an intellectual community committed to rigorous research, dialogue, and the transmission of a shared political and historical legacy among Spain, Portugal, and Latin America.
MisionVision.vision: To become an international reference in the study of Hispanic political thought and the tradition of freedom, helping this intellectual heritage illuminate contemporary challenges. We aspire to strengthen bonds among Spanish- and Portuguese-speaking nations through a shared understanding of their history, political philosophy, and future projection.

FAQ.a1: Yes, at any time from your user area. Cancellation takes effect at the end of the contracted period.
FAQ.q2: How does the academic admission process work?
FAQ.a2: The committee evaluates your CV and unpublished text. We look for rigor and alignment with the School's values. The acceptance ratio guarantees the quality of the community.

Meta.title: Biographies | Escuela Hispánica
Meta.description: History of the Escuela Hispánica through its fundamental authors.

intro.p1: Over the centuries, the cultural space formed by the Hispanic Monarchy and the Iberian world developed a singular intellectual tradition: a reflection on freedom that was not born from revolutionary rupture, but from the legal and moral elaboration of power. Since the 16th century, the universities of Salamanca, Coimbra, and Évora articulated a community that thought about politics based on natural law and the structural limitation of authority.
intro.p2: This political language crossed the Atlantic and was transformed in America. Hispanic American independences did not mean the abandonment of that tradition, but its evolution toward republican constitutionalism. Unity ceased to be territorial and became civilizational: a language, a law, and a shared conception of power as service to the common good remained shared on both sides of the ocean.
intro.p3: The Hispanic tradition of freedom is not a marginal current, but one of the pillars of the West. It is a different path from contractualist enlightenment, but convergent in its results: the affirmation that freedom is a moral order prior to political power.
intro.p4: Below, we present a continuous temporal horizon where ideas appear, are transmitted, and are transformed, from the 16th-century Iberian School to contemporary thought.

vitoria.name: Francisco de Vitoria
vitoria.shortBio: Dominican from Burgos and professor in Salamanca, he renewed theology after studying in Paris.
vitoria.contribution: Founder of modern international law; defended the natural rights of indigenous people and sovereignty based on reason.

lascasas.name: Bartolomé de las Casas
lascasas.shortBio: Dominican and bishop, he dedicated his life to the legal defense of the indigenous people of the Americas.
lascasas.contribution: Proposed universal legal equality and argued that civil power belongs to the people, regardless of their religion.

azpilcueta.name: Martín de Azpilcueta
azpilcueta.shortBio: The 'Doctor Navarrus', a central figure in Salamanca and Coimbra.
azpilcueta.contribution: First formulated the quantity theory of money, linking its value to scarcity and criticizing monetary manipulation.

soto.name: Domingo de Soto
soto.shortBio: Disciple of Vitoria and imperial theologian at the Council of Trent.
soto.contribution: Architect of natural law; formulated the theory of just price based on common market estimation.

veracruz.name: Alonso de la Vera Cruz
veracruz.shortBio: Augustinian and the first systematic philosopher of America, founder of the University of Mexico.
veracruz.contribution: Defended the legitimate sovereignty of indigenous peoples by natural law and political self-determination.

covarrubias.name: Diego de Covarrubias y Leyva
covarrubias.shortBio: Great jurist of the Renaissance and member of the Council of Castile.
covarrubias.contribution: Developed the subjective theory of value based on human perception and defended the autonomy of the civil sphere.

menchaca.name: Fernando Vázquez de Menchaca
menchaca.shortBio: Avant-garde secular jurist, influential throughout Europe.
menchaca.contribution: Affirmed that power is born from a rational pact among free individuals, anticipating popular sovereignty.

mercado.name: Tomás de Mercado
mercado.shortBio: Dominican from Seville who analyzed the global economy between Europe and America.
mercado.contribution: Legitimized trade based on transparency and informed consent; precursor of modern contractual ethics.

medina.name: Bartolomé de Medina
medina.shortBio: Dominican in Salamanca, renewer of moral methodology.
medina.contribution: Formulated moral probabilism, granting autonomy to individual conscience against authority.

anchieta.name: José de Anchieta
anchieta.shortBio: Canarian Jesuit in Brazil, founder of Brazilian colonial society.
anchieta.contribution: Implemented subsidiary political structures and organized communities respecting local cultural forms.

molina.name: Luis de Molina
molina.shortBio: Jesuit from Cuenca in Coimbra, defender of human freedom.
molina.contribution: Defined the market as a moral institution where price arises from free agreement, linking economy and freedom.

fonseca.name: Pedro da Fonseca
fonseca.shortBio: The 'Portuguese Aristotle' of Coimbra, master of Iberian Aristotelianism.
fonseca.contribution: Established the metaphysical basis of natural law, subordinating politics to the rational order of nature.

banez.name: Domingo Báñez
banez.shortBio: Dominican from Valladolid and a great theologian of the Counter-Reformation.
banez.contribution: Defended the objective limits of political power; authority does not create law, but must reflect it.

mariana.name: Juan de Mariana
mariana.shortBio: Radical Jesuit from Talavera, defender of the right of resistance.
mariana.contribution: Affirmed that power resides in the community; criticized inflation as theft and justified the deposition of the tyrant.

aragon.name: Pedro de Aragón
aragon.shortBio: Dominican from Salamanca, systematizer of the Second Scholasticism.
aragon.contribution: Conceptualized property as a social function and limited the prince's authority over subjects' rights.

suarez.name: Francisco Suárez
suarez.shortBio: The 'Doctor Eximius', a Jesuit and the last great scholastic metaphysician.
suarez.contribution: Systematized the theory of delegated power: sovereignty resides in the people and the ruler is a fiduciary administrator.

acosta.name: José de Acosta
acosta.shortBio: Jesuit and missionary in Peru and Mexico, pioneer of anthropology.
acosta.contribution: Defended indigenous political rationality and the moral unity of humanity against cultural diversity.

belarmino.name: Roberto Belarmino
belarmino.shortBio: Jesuit cardinal and a great European polemicist of the Counter-Reformation.
belarmino.contribution: Held that civil authority is mediate and derived from popular consent, influencing constitutionalism.

salas.name: Juan de Salas
salas.shortBio: Jesuit from Salamanca, expert in distributive justice and contracts.
salas.contribution: Defined the market as an order of norms based on rational trust and legal certainty.

solorzano.name: Juan de Solórzano Pereira
solorzano.shortBio: Jurista and judge in Lima, systematizer of Indian law.
solorzano.contribution: Proposed a legal pluralism where communities retain their norms under superior principles of justice.

lugo.name: Juan de Lugo
lugo.shortBio: Jesuit cardinal and a great economist of late scholasticism.
lugo.contribution: Formulated a subjective theory of value based on estimated utility, anticipating marginalist economics.

palafox.name: Juan de Palafox y Mendoza
palafox.shortBio: Bishop of Puebla and Viceroy of New Spain, institutional reformer.
palafox.contribution: Defended authority as a fiduciary office and the moral responsibility of the ruler to the community.

caramuel.name: Juan Caramuel y Lobkowitz
caramuel.shortBio: Cistercian and Baroque polymath, expert in logic and probability.
caramuel.contribution: Applied probabilistic reasoning to morals and politics, viewing the social order as a complex system.

velarde.name: Pedro Murillo Velarde
velarde.shortBio: Jesuit in the Philippines, systematizer of law overseas.
velarde.contribution: Applied the Salamanca tradition to the Asian context, defending a universal legal order based on natural law.

campomanes.name: Pedro Rodríguez de Campomanes
campomanes.shortBio: Economist and prosecutor of the Council of Castile, enlightened reformer.
campomanes.contribution: Defended productive freedom against monopolies, seeing the economic order as a spontaneous social phenomenon.

clavijero.name: Francisco Javier Clavijero
clavijero.shortBio: Exiled New Spanish Jesuit, defender of American culture.
clavijero.contribution: Articulated a philosophical defense of indigenous political rationality, laying foundations for American autonomy.

jovellanos.name: Gaspar Melchor de Jovellanos
jovellanos.shortBio: Great Spanish Enlightenment figure, magistrate and defender of classic natural law.
jovellanos.contribution: Defended that institutions sprout from social life and that private property is a moral pillar of civilization.

viscardo.name: Juan Pablo Viscardo y Guzmán
viscardo.shortBio: Peruvian Jesuit and the first great ideologue of emancipation.
viscardo.contribution: Transformed scholastic pactism into independence consciousness: if power does not serve the common good, the bond is broken.

marina.name: Francisco Martínez Marina
marina.shortBio: Priest and historian, defender of historical constitutionalism.
marina.contribution: Argued that national sovereignty in Cadiz was the recovery of a Spanish historical pact for the limitation of power.

forner.name: Juan Pablo Forner
forner.shortBio: Salamanca jurist and prosecutor, critic of abstract rationalism.
forner.contribution: Defended that law is born from sedimented social practice and not from invented universal theories.

miranda.name: Francisco de Miranda
miranda.shortBio: 'The Precursor', Venezuelan military and thinker of Atlantic reach.
miranda.contribution: Synthesized scholastic popular sovereignty with modern constitutionalism to design American freedom.

talamantes.name: Melchor de Talamantes
talamantes.shortBio: Peruvian Mercedarian in Mexico, jurist of sovereignty.
talamantes.contribution: Applied Suárez's doctrine to the crisis of 1808: power returns to the people when legitimate authority disappears.

mier.name: Servando Teresa de Mier
mier.shortBio: Radical Mexican Dominican, bridge between scholasticism and republic.
mier.contribution: Used Suárez's doctrine to justify independence as a restoration of the natural legal order.

henriquez.name: Camilo Henríquez
henriquez.shortBio: Chilean priest and journalist, disseminator of political doctrine.
henriquez.contribution: Adapted Suarecian pactism to popular language, legitimizing self-government as a demand of social nature.

bello.name: Andrés Bello
bello.shortBio: Universal humanist, author of the Chilean Civil Code.
bello.contribution: Stabilized republics by integrating the Hispanic legal tradition with modern rationality and legal certainty.

alaman.name: Lucas Alamán
alaman.shortBio: Mexican historian and politician, defender of the institutional order.
alaman.contribution: Affirmed that freedom requires a prior moral foundation, criticizing abstract sovereignty without limits.

estrada.name: José María Gutiérrez de Estrada
estrada.shortBio: Mexican diplomat and jurist, critic of political instability.
estrada.contribution: Proposed institutions that would ensure the neutrality of power and its subordination to the common good.

herrera.name: Bartolomé Herrera
herrera.shortBio: Peruvian priest and jurist, rector at San Carlos de Lima.
herrera.contribution: Transferred Suárez's doctrine to the republic, defending that sovereignty must be oriented toward the common good.

cortes.name: Juan Donoso Cortés
cortes.shortBio: Diplomat and thinker from Extremadura, analyst of the European crisis.
cortes.contribution: Warned that without natural law or a moral foundation, politics degenerates into anarchy or state absolutism.

herculano.name: Alexandre Herculano
herculano.shortBio: Portuguese historian and politician, defender of historical freedoms.
herculano.contribution: Recovered the tradition of municipalities and charters as the origin of constitutionalism against the Jacobin model.

balmes.name: Jaime Balmes
balmes.shortBio: Priest and philosopher, a great systematizer of 19th-century Spanish Catholic thought.
balmes.contribution: Argued that European freedoms were born from the Christian conception of the person and the limitation of power.

menendez.name: Marcelino Menéndez Pelayo
menendez.shortBio: Historian and polygraph from Santander, he reconstructed the intellectual continuity of Spain.
menendez.contribution: Rescued the scholastics as central authors of modernity and returned historical consciousness to the Hispanic tradition.

mella.name: Juan Vázquez de Mella
mella.shortBio: Principal theorist of traditionalism, he converted pactism into modern political theory.
mella.contribution: Defended a society of historical communities where authority is delegated by the living community and is not absolute.

rizal.name: José Rizal
rizal.shortBio: National hero and Filipino thinker, moral critic of arbitrary power.
rizal.contribution: Demanded rights in the name of Hispanic legality, arguing that authority loses legitimacy when it violates justice.

mabini.name: Apolinario Mabini
mabini.shortBio: Theorist of Philippine independence, advisor to President Aguinaldo.
mabini.contribution: Elaborated a theory of the State based on moral sovereignty: government exists for the common good and is limited by justice.

unamuno.name: Miguel de Unamuno
unamuno.shortBio: Philosopher and rector in Salamanca, defender of conscience against mechanism.
unamuno.contribution: Recovered the scholastic anthropological core: politics must serve the real man and conscience must prevail over power.

maeztu.name: Ramiro de Maeztu
maeztu.shortBio: Basque thinker of international reach, defender of Hispanidad.
maeztu.contribution: Interpreted Hispanidad as a legal civilization centered on the dignity of the person and natural law.

belaunde.name: Víctor Andrés Belaúnde
belaunde.shortBio: Peruvian diplomat and essayist, president of the UN General Assembly.
belaunde.contribution: Defined Hispanic political culture as personalist, integrating human rights with roots in historical communities.

ortega.name: José Ortega y Gasset
ortega.shortBio: The most influential Spanish philosopher of the 20th century, renewer of European thought.
ortega.contribution: Understood that the State does not create society but organizes it, prioritizing social life over political structure.

madariaga.name: Salvador de Madariaga
madariaga.shortBio: Galician diplomat and humanist, interpreter of universal consciousness.
madariaga.contribution: Placed the origin of modern human rights in scholastic moral doubts about the legitimacy of conquest.

sardinha.name: António Sardinha
sardinha.shortBio: Portuguese thinker and ideologue of Lusitanian Integralism.
sardinha.contribution: Defended that Iberian civilization did not know absolute sovereignty, with power always subordinated to society.

moncada.name: Cabral de Moncada
moncada.shortBio: Great philosopher of law in Coimbra, critic of legal positivism.
moncada.contribution: Recovered the principle that the legislator does not create law, but must recognize an objective order of justice.

meinvielle.name: Julio Meinvielle
meinvielle.shortBio: Argentine priest and philosopher, defender of Thomistic realism.
meinvielle.contribution: Affirmed that politics must facilitate a virtuous life and that freedom is the rational order of human action.

eyzaguirre.name: Jaime Eyzaguirre
eyzaguirre.shortBio: Chilean historian, re-interpreter of American independence.
eyzaguirre.contribution: Showed that emancipation was an activation of scholastic popular sovereignty and not a contractual rupture.

grice.name: Marjorie Grice-Hutchinson
grice.shortBio: British economist, rediscoverer of the School of Salamanca.
grice.contribution: Demonstrated that Spanish scholastics formulated the principles of a free economic order centuries before Adam Smith.

navarro.name: Mariano Navarro Rubio
navarro.shortBio: Spanish Minister of Finance, promoter of the 1959 Stabilization Plan.
navarro.contribution: Applied the principle of monetary stability and fiscal responsibility as guarantees of civil freedom.

gongora.name: Mario Góngora
gongora.shortBio: Great historian of the Chilean State, disciple of historical realism.
gongora.contribution: Held that the State arises from a prior society and that its legitimacy depends on respecting the community's history.

dors.name: Álvaro d’Ors
dors.shortBio: Prestigious Romanist and philosopher of law, defender of social authority.
dors.contribution: Distinguished between authority (social) and potestas (political), warning that state absolutism is born by replacing law.

stoetzer.name: Carlos Stoetzer
stoetzer.shortBio: Jurist and researcher of the scholastic roots of constitutionalism.
stoetzer.contribution: Demonstrated that popular sovereignty and limited government in the Americas proceed from the matrix of Vitoria and Suárez.

termes.name: Rafael Termes Carreró
termes.shortBio: Economist and thinker, defender of humanism in capitalism.
termes.contribution: Linked the market economy with personal responsibility and the natural law tradition.

canals.name: Francisco Canals Vidal
canals.shortBio: Philosopher from Barcelona and Thomistic professor, a central figure in Christian personalism.
canals.contribution: Defended that society is born from the natural sociability of the person and that power must serve the common good.

sanchez.name: Ismael Sánchez Bella
sanchez.shortBio: Great historian of Indian law at the University of Navarra.
sanchez.contribution: Demonstrated that the Hispanic Monarchy in America functioned as a plural and jurisdictional legal order.

mora.name: Gonzalo Fernández de la Mora
mora.shortBio: Diplomat and political thinker, author of 'The Twilight of Ideologies'.
mora.contribution: Defended a rational policy subordinated to the objective common good, referring to natural law.

velardefuertes.name: Juan Velarde Fuertes
velardefuertes.shortBio: Professor and economist from Asturias, expert in the history of Spanish thought.
velardefuertes.contribution: Reintegrated the School of Salamanca into the history of economics, highlighting its moral analysis of the market.

gonzalez.name: Efraín González Morfín
gonzalez.shortBio: Mexican intellectual and politician, exponent of Christian humanism.
gonzalez.contribution: Defended that the State does not create rights but recognizes them, organizing the common good according to human nature.

negro.name: Dalmacio Negro Pavón
negro.shortBio: Professor and political philosopher from Madrid, analyst of the transformation of power.
negro.contribution: Distinguished between social authority and autonomous state power, vindicating classic moral limits.

sacheri.name: Carlos Alberto Sacheri
sacheri.shortBio: Argentine philosopher and university professor at UCA.
sacheri.contribution: Criticized the claim to unlimited sovereignty of the modern State, defending that power is fiduciary.

ochaa.name: Constantino Ocha’a Mve Bengobesama
ochaa.shortBio: Equatoguinean jurist trained in Spain, defender of the rule of law.
ochaa.contribution: Proposed a State based on rational norms and recognition of the community against personal power.

schwartz.name: Pedro Schwartz
schwartz.shortBio: Economist and academic from Madrid, defender of classical liberalism.
schwartz.contribution: Highlighted the institutional continuity of Spanish charters and pactism as the origin of Hispanic freedoms.

bravo.name: Bernardino Bravo Lira
bravo.shortBio: Chilean jurist and historian, researcher of jurisdictional law.
bravo.contribution: Demonstrated that American constitutionalism inherits the medieval and scholastic tradition of power limitation.

alvira.name: Rafael Alvira
alvira.shortBio: Professor from Zaragoza, defender of realism in social philosophy.
alvira.contribution: Developed a theory of society centered on the community as a reality prior to the State.

nogueira.name: Jaime Nogueira Pinto
nogueira.shortBio: Portuguese essayist and historian, analyst of political legitimacy.
nogueira.contribution: Vindicated authority limited by traditions and intermediate bodies against absolute sovereignty.

brito.name: António José de Brito
brito.shortBio: Portuguese political philosopher, specialist in classic natural law.
brito.contribution: Defended that Western civilization can only sustain itself if it recognizes moral limits on power.

cabrillo.name: Francisco Cabrillo Rodríguez
cabrillo.shortBio: Professor of applied economics, expert in economic analysis of law.
cabrillo.contribution: Linked economic freedom with the Hispanic institutional tradition of arbitrary power limitation.

moreira.name: José Manuel Moreira
moreira.shortBio: Portuguese philosopher and economist, researcher of economic ethics.
moreira.contribution: Placed the Second Scholasticism in the genealogy of Western liberalism, breaking the Anglo-Saxon narrative.

ndongo.name: Donato Ndongo-Bidyogo
ndongo.shortBio: Equatoguinean intellectual and novelist, critic of autonomous power.
ndongo.contribution: Reflected on personal dignity as a reality prior to the State and on the limits of authority.

belda.name: Juan Belda Plans
belda.shortBio: Priest and historian of theology, expert on the School of Salamanca.
belda.contribution: Reconstructed the School of Salamanca as an intellectual program that responded to global challenges.

huerta.name: Jesús Huerta de Soto
huerta.shortBio: Professor of political economy and defender of the Austrian School.
huerta.contribution: Demonstrated that modern liberalism is a coherent development of scholastic natural law.
